coredata.dc:title: Luminescence of K2BaCa(PO4)2: Ce3+, Tb3+ dual-emitting phosphor and its application as ratiometric optical thermometer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Mo, Fuwang
Zhang, Xinguo
Sun, Zishan
Zhu, Zhenpeng
Guo, Ziying
Wu, Zhan-chao
coredata.dc:description: A new dual-emitting phosphor K2BaCa(PO4)2: Ce3+, Tb3+ (KBCP: Ce3+, Tb3+) was successfully synthesized and designed to put into application of ratiometric temperature sensing. Under single UV excitation, the developed phosphors shows both Ce3+ violet and Tb3+ green emission mainly at 360 and 542 nm, respectively. The Ce3+-Tb3+ energy transfer process was analyzed by the variation of emission spectra and Ce3+/Tb3+ decay lifetimes. The remarkable thermal stability has also been tested, indicating that the intensity at 200 °C is still above 85% of the original intensity. Furthermore, the emission intensity ratio between 360 and 542 nm of KBCP: Ce3+, Tb3+ is found to have a good linear relationship with temperature (R 2 > 0.993), and the corresponding sensitivity temperature resolution is 0.35% K−1 and >0.02 K, respectively. Thus, this novel dual-emitting phosphor can be used as a potential luminescent thermometer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: K2BaCa(PO4)2
Luminescence
Energy transfer
Ratiometric thermometer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of K2BaCa(PO4)2: 0.03Ce3+, yTb3+ phosphors with its standard card (CSD-434656).
The crystal structure of K2BaCa(PO4)2 and the coordinated condition of cations in K2BaCa(PO4)2 (K+, Ba2+, Ca2+, P5+).
PLE and PL spectra of KBCP: 0.03Ce3+, KBCP: 0.03 Tb3+ (a), and KBCP: 0.03Ce3+, 0.03 Tb3+ (b).

PL spectra of KBCP: 0.03Ce3+, yTb3+ phosphors under 310 nm excitation, inset is the variation of Ce3+ and Tb3+ emission intensity (a); The CIE chromaticity diagrams of KBCP: 0.03Ce3+, yTb3+ phosphors (b).
Decay curves of Ce3+ (a) and Tb3+ emission (b) in KBCP: 0.03Ce3+, yTb3+ phosphors after pulse excitation at 320 nm while monitored at 360 and 542 nm.
The possible energy transfer scheme in K2BaCa(PO4)2: Ce3+, Tb3+ system.

Temperature-dependent PL spectra of KBCP: 0.03Ce3+, 0.03 Tb3+ at 310 nm excitation (a), the variation of normalized integrated intensity for total, Ce3+ and Tb3+ emission (b), linear fitting of I Ce/I Tb versus different temperature (c).
Variation of relative sensitivity (a) and temperature resolution (b) in range from 293 K to 473 K.

head.ce:title: Luminescence of K2BaCa(PO4)2: Ce3+, Tb3+ dual-emitting phosphor and its application as ratiometric optical thermometer

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Guangxi Key Laboratory of Calcium Carbonate Resources Comprehensive Utilization, College of Materials & Chemical Engineering, Hezhou University, Hezhou, 542899, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Calcium Carbonate Resources Comprehensive Utilization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new dual-emitting phosphor K2BaCa(PO4)2: Ce3+, Tb3+ (KBCP: Ce3+, Tb3+) was successfully synthesized and designed to put into application of ratiometric temperature sensing. Under single UV excitation, the developed phosphors shows both Ce3+ violet and Tb3+ green emission mainly at 360 and 542 nm, respectively. The Ce3+-Tb3+ energy transfer process was analyzed by the variation of emission spectra and Ce3+/Tb3+ decay lifetimes. The remarkable thermal stability has also been tested, indicating that the intensity at 200 °C is still above 85% of the original intensity. Furthermore, the emission intensity ratio between 360 and 542 nm of KBCP: Ce3+, Tb3+ is found to have a good linear relationship with temperature (R 2 > 0.993), and the corresponding sensitivity temperature resolution is 0.35% K−1 and >0.02 K, respectively. Thus, this novel dual-emitting phosphor can be used as a potential luminescent thermometer.

ce:section.ce:para: An accurate temperature measurement is of great important since temperature is the most essential parameter in everyday life and multiple industrial fields. Conventional thermometers such as expansion thermometer, thermocouple thermometer and Infrared thermometer could not satisfy the future measurement requirements of non-contact, high resolution and fast response [1–6]. Thus, a novel kind of temperature measurement method based on the temperature-dependent optical properties of the luminescent materials has garnered a lot of attention [7].

In a certain temperature range, the optical properties of luminescent materials, including peak position, fluorescence intensity, spectral line width, fluorescence intensity ratio, polarization anisotropy and fluorescence decay lifetime, vary with the change of temperature [8]. Therefore, we can use the variation of these optical properties to calibrate temperature. Among various luminescence thermometry methods, the one based on the fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) is considered to have the greatest potential for optical thermometry application. This is because that the accuracy of FIR method will not be affected by experimental conditions like the fluorescence loss, the excitation power fluctuation or the concentration of luminescence centers, while exhibits high resolution and small measurement error [9,10].

Dual-emitting color-tunable luminescent material using sensitizer-activator energy transfer scheme could be a good candidate as ratiometric temperature sensors [11]. Utilizing the thermal-sensitive emission intensity ratio in a single-host dual-emitting phosphor, ratiometric optical thermometers with excellent stability, broad sensing range and high sensitivity have been constructed and reported by our team [12]. β-Ca3(PO4)2 is of whitlockite structure, in which The Ca(1), Ca(2), Ca(3), and Ca(5) sites are fully occupied and Ca(4) site is half occupied by calcium and vacancy. Using crystal-site engineering strategy, many new kinds of lattices and phosphors with β-Ca3(PO4)2 structure can be derived when the Ca(4) site is fully occupied or completely empty [13–15]. Eu2+-doped K2BaCa(PO4)2 is reported as as a iso-Ca3(PO4)2 structural phosphate and an efficient cyan-emitting phosphor with excellent thermal stability [16]. It is expected that the K2BaCa(PO4)2 codoped with Ce3+ and Tb3+ show intense, bright green emission under ultraviolet irradiation with high quantum efficiency. In this work, the luminescent properties of Ce3+, Tb3+ co-doped K2BaCa(PO4)2 (KBCP) phosphor were systematically investigated. The Ce3+ violet emission and Tb3+ green emission shows different temperature-dependent variation tendency, which results in a linear-correlated FIR value with temperature, thus can be exploited as potential luminescent thermometer.

A series of K2-x-y BaCa(PO4)2: xCe3+, yTb3+ with x = 0.03 and y = 0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.03, 0.06, 0.09, 0.12, 0.15 and 0.18 phosphors were prepared by the conventional solid-state reaction. Raw materials such as K2CO3, BaCO3, CaCO3, NH4H2PO4, CeO2 and Tb4O7 were weighted and grounded for 10 min. Subsequently, the products were transferred into a tube furnace and sintered twice at 1200 °C in 95% N2–5% H2 reducing atmosphere for 4 h. Then, the obtained samples were naturally cooled and grounded to get final phosphors.

The phase purity of all samples were checked by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using D/max-2000 powder diffractometer. The steady and transient spectra for phosphors were measured by FLS-980 spectrometer. The excitation source for PL/PLE spectra, Ce3+ and Tb3+ lifetime is 60 W Xe-arc lamp, 320 nm UV laser diode and 25 W Xe-flash lamp attached to FLS-980 spectrometer, respectively. To figure out the temperature-dependent PL property, emission spectra in the range of 293–473 K with internal of 10 K were detected by using FLS-980 spectrometer with a liquid nitrogen cryostat (OptistatDN). The duration of thermal treatment for PL spectrum at each temperature point is about 5 min. For example, after temperature reaches at 473 K, it takes 3 min to stabilize the temperature in range of 473 ± 0.1 K, then 2 min to finish the corresponding PL measurement.

The XRD patterns of the representative phosphors are listed in Fig. 1 , in which all diffraction peaks are matched with standard card (CSD-434656), indicating the substitution of Ce3+ and Tb3+ has no obvious change of host structure. It is known that K2BaCa(PO4)2 belongs to the P-3m1(164) space group with a trigonal structure. As shown in Fig. 2 , the seven-coordinated cationic site is evenly occupied by potassium/calcium ions and the K0.5Ca0.5O7 site has a series of bond lengths from 2.65 to 2.80 Å. Besides, potassium ion also occupies an octahedron site with the K-O bond length of 2.233 Å. A larger octahedron is also observed as the BaO6 polyhedron with Ba-O bond length of 2.9015 Å. As revealed by Xia et al. [16], DFT calculations indicates that most Eu2+ dopant in K2BaCa(PO4)2 are located at KO6 sites of KBCP due to the obvious lower defect formation energy than other cationic sites, which was supported by the results from Rietveld refinements. Because of the similarity of valence and ionic radius of Eu2+, Ce3+ and Tb3+, similar scenario could be applied in KBCP: Ce3+, Tb3+ system. Hence, both Ce3+ and Tb3+ ions are expected to locate in KO6 site of the K2BaCa(PO4)2 matrix.

The excitation and emission (PLE & PL) spectra of representative Ce3+/Tb3+-doped and Ce3+, Tb3+ co-doped KBCP phosphors are shown in Fig. 3 . When monitoring at 360 nm emission peak, the PLE spectrum of KBCP: 0.03Ce3+ exhibits a single absorption band centered at 310 nm, which covers the range from 250 to 350 nm and could be attributed to Ce3+ 4f-5d allowed transition. The corresponding PL spectrum shows an asymmetric purple broad band with peak wavelength at 360 nm. Using Gaussian fitting, the asymmetric emission band could be deconvolved into two components with the peak at 347 and 372 nm, along with the calculated energy difference of 1936 cm−1. Meanwhile, the theoretical energy difference value between 2 F 7/2 and 2 F 5/2 ground state is ∼2000 cm−1. Accordingly, those two deconvolved peaks are assigned to the transitions from Ce3+ 5d excited states to spin-orbits splitting of the 4f ground state [17,18]. The PLE spectrum of KBCP: 0.03 Tb3+ monitored at 542 nm displays one 4f-5d broadband in 200–300 nm range and a series of 4f-4f sharp peaks in 300 and 450 nm range. The PL spectrum of KBCP: 0.03 Tb3+ consists of a series of weak emission peaks at 488, 542, 585 and 625 nm due to spin-forbidden 5 D 4→7 F 6,5,4,3 transitions of Tb3+. It is clearly shown that there is a significant overlap between Ce3+ emission spectrum and the Tb3+ excitation spectrum, indicating effective energy transfer can be achieved from Ce3+ to Tb3+ in K2BaCa(PO4)2.

Fig. 3b illustrates the PLE spectra of Ce3+/Tb3+ co-doped KBCP sample monitored at Ce3+ 370 nm emission and Tb3+ 542 nm emission. Ce3+ broad absorption band peaked at 310 nm can clearly be observed in both excitation spectra, which indicates the occurrence of Ce3+-Tb3+ energy transfer. The Ce3+/Tb3+ co-doped phosphor emits intense green light peaked at 542 nm upon 310 nm excitation, corresponding to the sensitized Tb3+ 4f-4f transitions. Co-doping of Ce3+ ions in K2BaCa(PO4)2: Tb3+ brings to a prominent enhancement in the green Tb3+ emission, which is ascribed to boosted absorption efficiency by sensitizer Ce3+ 4f-5d allowed transitions and followed efficient Ce3+-Tb3+ energy transfer.

The effect of the Tb3+ concentration on the PL properties of KBCP: 0.03Ce3+, yTb3+ is shown in Fig. 4 a. The increasing Tb3+ doping content results in the gradually decreased Ce3+ luminescence along with rising Tb3+ emission, while their peak wavelengths keep unchanged. As shown in Fig. 4 inset, a significant enhancement of Tb3+ green emission intensity is observed when its doping concentration rises to 0.15, followed by a reduction of emission intensity at higher Tb3+ concentration. The decline of Tb3+ emission at high concentration (y = 0.18) is due to the occurrence of intra-Tb3+ concentration quenching. Thus, the energy transfer efficiency (η
ET ) in KBCP: 0.03Ce3+, yTb3+ phosphors could be obtained by the variation of sensitizer's emission intensity with and without activator (I
s and I
s0) [19]:
η
E T
= 1 −
I
s
I
s 0

Thus, the value of η
ET is calculated to be 10.5%, 19.5%, 25.6%, 37.9%, 52.6%, 66.5%, 76.7% and 80.1% for y = 0.005, 0.01, 0.03, 0.06, 0.09, 0.12, 0.15 and 0.18, which increases gradually with increasing the Tb3+ concentration and reach the maximum at y = 0.18. As a comparison, the η
ET value for Na3Sc2P3O12: Ce3+, Tb3+ [20], Li2Ca4Si4O13: Ce3+, Tb3+ [21] and K2MgSi3O8: Ce3+, Tb3+ [22] is 76.63%, 45% and 51%, respectively. For KBCP: Ce3+, Tb3+ at varied Tb3+ contents (y), their CIE chromaticity diagrams is shown in Fig. 4b. The color point evolves from violet (0.211, 0.119) to green (0.333, 0.570), which results from the ET processes of Ce3+→Tb3+.

To shed light on concentration quenching process, the critical distance (R c) can be further calculated by Refs. [23,24]:
R
c
= 2
(
3 V
4 π
x
c
N
)
1 / 3

Where the parameters of V, x
c and N stands for the unit cell volume, critical concentration and the dopant occupied sites in the unit cell, respectively. In present case of K2BaCa(PO4)2 host, V = 202.09 Å3, x c = 0.15, N = 2. Therefore, the calculated R c value is 10.87 Å. In general, exchange interaction and multipolar-multipolar interactions are the resonant ET mechanisms. It is well-known that the general critical distance for exchange interchange is 5 Å or less. With obvious larger R c value (∼11 Å), exchange interaction is not possibly responsible for present concentration quenching phenomenon, and electric multipolar interaction should be the dominant mechanism.

Luminescent decay time is a good path to study sensitizer-activator energy transfer process. Under 320 nm laser excitation, sensitizer Ce3+ decay curves of KBCP: 0.03Ce3+, yTb3+ phosphors monitored at 360 nm are shown in Fig. 5 a, which are fitted with a bi-exponential equation:
I
( t )
=
I
0
+
A
1
exp
( −
t
τ
1
)
+
A
2
exp
( −
t
τ
2
)
where I(t) and I 0 represents the emission intensity at certain time (t and 0), A 1 and A 2 is known as pre-exponential factors, τ 1 and τ 2 stands for fitting components. In order to quantitatively evaluate Ce3+ lifetime variation, its average lifetime (τ ∗) is calculated using A 1, A 2 and τ 1, τ 2 [25]:
τ
∗
=
(
A
1
τ
1
2
+
A
2
τ
2
2
)
/
(
A
1
τ
1
+
A
2
τ
2
)

The average lifetime for the Ce3+ emission is calculated to be 28.70, 27.68, 26.28, 22.99, 21.88, 19.17, 16.85, 15.30 and 14.67 ns for y = 0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.03, 0.06, 0.09, 0.12, 0.15 and 0.18, respectively. The Ce3+ lifetime undergoes a reduction process with rising Tb3+ content due to the duration time of electrons on excited states is shorten with efficient energy transfer.

As a comparison, the Tb3+ decay curve exhibit the emission rise time at y = 0.05–0.03, which is defined as an immediate increase of the emission intensity after the excitation pulse and prior to the emission decay. The emission rise time in 0–1 ms range could be attributed to the populated Tb3+ 5 D 3,4 excited states by Ce3+-Tb3+ energy transfer. For these states, transitions to the 7 F
J ground states are spin-forbidden resulting in lower decay rates and thus prolonged fluorescence lifetimes. As shown in Fig. 4b, the emission rise time generally disappears along with rising Tb3+ content, this is due to a faster depopulation of Tb3+ excited states with increasing intra-Tb3+ energy transfer. The average lifetime of Tb3+ emission is found to be 3.088, 3.072, 3.062, 3.040, 2.969, 2.907, 2.881 and 2.830 ms for y = 0.005, 0.01, 0.03, 0.06, 0.09, 0.12, 0.15 and 0.18, respectively. .

A schematic diagram is presented in Fig. 6 to demonstrate the Ce3+-Tb3+ energy transfer process in K2BaCa(PO4)2. Ce3+ electrons absorb UV excitation energy and jump from 4f ground state to 5d excited state. A fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) occurs due to the energetic similarity of Ce3+ 5d state and Tb3+ 5 D 3 state. Subsequently, the Tb3+ excited electron are relaxes to the 5 D 4 excited levels by a nonradiative process, then follows by radiative 5 D 4-7 F
J transition and results in the characteristic Tb3+ green emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability and FIR temperature sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Both temperature-dependent spectral shape and intensity have significant influence on the performance of luminescent thermometer. Different temperature response of dual emission is essential to realize a ratiometric thermometer. The temperature-dependent PL spectra of optimized phosphor KBCP: 0.03Ce3+, 0.03 Tb3+ is measured and shown in Fig. 7 a. No obvious change could be found in the overall shape and peak wavelength of PL spectra from 293 to 473 K. The Fig. 7b shows the normalized integrated intensity change depending on increasing temperature. It is observed that Ce3+ violet emission intensity undergoes a gradually decrease as the temperature increases, indicating its non-radiative relaxation is accelerated by thermal-enhanced energy transfer effect, i.e. The rising temperature offers extra energy to the excited electrons located at the Ce3+ excited state, which would transfer to Tb3+ 5 D 4 state and then relax to the bottom of 5 D 4 state, and result in sensitized green emission. Similar phenomena have been reported in LaOBr: Ce3+, Tb3+ and Ca9La(GeO4)0.75(PO4)6: Ce3+,Tb3+ [26–28]. Additionally, as a quantitative analysis, the Ce3+ integrated emission intensity at 373 K drop 12% compared to the initial intensity at room temperature, and even at 473 K, the integrated emission intensity still remains over 73% of that in room temperature. Meanwhile, Tb3+ integrated emission intensity shows a reverse temperature-dependent tendency, which rises gradually and reaches to 116% of initial intensity at 473 K. The observed enhancement of Tb3+ green emission could be assigned to the thermal population of Tb3+ 4f excited levels by the rising Ce3+-Tb3+ energy transfer probability at high temperature. The total emission intensity at 473 K retains 89% of the initial value, indicating the excellent thermal stability of our developed phosphor. Since Ce3+ and Tb3+ emission intensity has different temperature responses, it is expected that our prepared phosphors can provide a good temperature sensing property.

Fig. 7c depicts the FIR values as function of temperature. The FIR value exhibits a declining tendency with rising temperature, reaching its minimum value of 1.18 at 473 K. A linear relationship between FIR and temperature (T) with correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9934 can be obtained using the following expression:
T = 485 − 254.8 ∗
I
C e
I
T b
,
R
2
= 0.9934
where I Ce and I Tb denote the integrated emission intensities of 320–450 nm and 450–700 nm range, respectively. Such a good linear fitting result demonstrated that the FIR value of KBCP: Ce3+, Tb3+ owns a promising temperature-response function, and eventually it can be used as a temperature indicator.

In order to assess the temperature-sensing property, the relative sensitivity (S
r ) and the temperature resolution (δT) is analyzed. S
r value could be obtained using the variation rate of FIR with changing temperature as [29]:
S
r
=
|
∂ F I R / ∂ T
F I R
|

The temperature-dependent S
r variation is shown in Fig. 8 a and its maximum data is 0.35% K−1 at 473 K. Meanwhile, the temperature resolution (δT) can be estimated by Refs. [30,31]:
δ T =
1
S
r
δ Δ
Δ
where
δ Δ / Δ
stands for the experimental relative error which is determined by measurement device. In present case, a signal to noise ratio of 20000: 1 is applied for FLS-980 spectrometer, which results in
δ Δ / Δ
of 0.01%. The δT value of KBCP: 0.03Ce3+, 0.03 Tb3+ versus T is displayed in Fig. 8b, δT is found to have a resolution high than a 0.02 K in 273–473 K range.

In summary, an efficient dual-emitting Ce3+, Tb3+ co-activated K2BaCa(PO4)2 phosphor is developed for temperature sensing application. XRD result suggests that Ce3+ and Tb3+ doping have no obvious change of host lattice. The PL spectra of the phosphors show dual-emission under single UV excitation, i.e. Ce3+ violet emission band at 360 nm and Tb3+ green emission peak at 542 nm. The temperature dependent PL emission spectra of the optimized phosphors revealed that the studied phosphors possess good thermal stability. The Ce3+ violet emission and Tb3+ green emission has distinct temperature response, i.e. the former shows quenching tendency and the latter exhibits rising tendency, respectively. This results in highly temperature-dependent luminescence intensity ratio. With high luminescence intensity, good index relationship between FIR and temperature (R 2 > 0.993), wide sensing range (293–473 K) with high resolution (>0.02 K), our developed phosphors could be a good candidate for ratiometric luminescent thermometer.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601081 and 51472132) and Guangzhou Scientific planning program (No. 201804010260).
